# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What's the most disturbing psychological thriller you've ever watched?

**Score:** 61
**Body:** I recently watched Obsession and Backrooms, and now I'm looking for more dark, disturbing, mind-bending thrillers.

&#x200B;

I'm not really looking for pure horror or jump scares. I enjoy movies that are tense, unsettling, psychologically intense, and leave you thinking about them afterward.

&#x200B;

What's a movie that genuinely shocked you, disturbed you, or stayed in your mind for days after watching it?

&#x200B;

&#x200B;

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. disturbing = nocturnal animals
2. Frailty
  - Adding Frailty to the list. The hidden gems are usually the ones that hit the hardest.
  - That was just damn awesome.
3. SE7EN
  - At this point, so many people have recommended me to watch Se7en that I'd be committing a crime by not watching it 😭
4. Cape Fear with De Niro is excellent
5. Funny Games

Se7en

Audition

Hush

Frailty

Primal Fear
6. martyrs 2008
7. Requiem of a dream

## 2. I love watching movies that are "Based on true events"

**Score:** 34
**Body:** Like everyone i have a variety of movies in my top list from heist genres **Oceans 11** and series to action thriller **Have Fallen** series. And there are all the Now you see me, Rocky, Speed, Jaws in the mix but there is nothing better than a movie that starts with "based on true events..." You cannot change my mind. So far this has been my watchlist -

1. The Blind Side
2. Erin Brockovich
3. Tigers (Nestlé baby milk scandal in Pakistan)
4. We Bought a Zoo
5. Pursuit of Happiness
6. Social Network

Can anyone help me grow this list? Thanks in advance.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dog Day Afternoon 
2. Schindler's List might be the best of them all
3. Argo
4. Catch Me if You Can 

I didn’t enjoy the Perfect Storm but it’s based on a real story 
5. Deep water Horizon

Only The Brave

Iron Claw, and Foxcatcher both about the wrestling world

Everest

The Big Short 

Hidden Figures 

Spotlight

6. Apollo 13
7. Spotlight 

Zodiac

Munich

Argo

Black Hawk Down 

13 Hours

Zero Dark Thirty 

Sully

Dunkirk
8. The Impossible 
9. The Wolf of Wall Street

Catch Me if You Can 

Boys Don’t Cry 

Lords of Dogtown 

Alpha Dog 

Gia 

The Normal Heart

I, Tonya 

The Big Short 
10. The Insider

Social Network

Battle of Algiers

Black Hawk Down

Zodiac

The Bank Job

61\*

United 93

## 3. Awakenings(1990)

**Score:** 21
**Body:** If anyone who had already watched ,please ignore , but what a film ! I was surfing through robin williams filmography,  found out this film, started it , thought it would be a feel good film but made me emotional at the end . We all know what a great actor robin williams is , but Robert Deniro ..man .. he nailed it ! If you are looking for an emotional , heart touching film , im suggesting you watch this ! 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I definitely agree.  I saw this movie as a kid and its one of the few movies that stayed with me.  It's based on a book by renowned Neurologist, Oliver Sacks and I would definitely recommend checking out some of his writings like "The Man Who Mistook His Wife for a Hat" and "An Anthropologist on Mars".  
  - I adore Sacks' books, and own An Anthropologist On Mars. 
  - I became a neurologist in part because of this movie.
  - I'd love too, thanks .must have been special for you  watching this movie in childhood . 
    - I love Awakenings, definitely leaves a lasting impact. May I also suggest [The Music Never Stopped \(2011\)](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/56401-the-music-never-stopped) It's based on Sacks' essay "The Last Hippie".
      - Noted ! Will watch it for sure .
2. In my top ten most emotional movies of all time. I think they nailed the perfect mix of humor and serious drama. I'm one of the few people who is not really a fan of De Niro, but he has perfectly cast for this role. God I miss Robin Williams.

You should watch One Hour Photo for another Robin Williams banger. It is a more subtle performance by him and I consider it an example of his best acting. He's realistically terrifying and creepy in the role. 
3. It is such an amazing movie! Incredibly emotional. Fantastic cast!
4. From a great book by Oliver Sacks. 
5. Fun fact: The Penelope Ann Miller role was added to shine over the fact that the real Leonard had a chronic masturbation issue about said Awakening. Not that I'm knocking the guy for it, but a fact nevertheless.

## 4. Remarkably Bright Creatures(2026)

**Score:** 20
**Body:** This was recommended to me. I have no expectation whatsover. It started as a light movie but ended to be a tear jerker one. A must watch! 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I loved the book so much and loved the movie as well.  Really great adaptation. 
2. I had the same experience. I really liked it.
3. I loved the movie tooo. 

Coda helped me scratch that itch

4. Cute movie
5. It was okay. Sally Fields carried it. It was like a Lifetime movie, but a good one.
6. More like Remarkably Boring Creatures 
  - Not enough fart jokes?

## 5. What animated movie from the past 5-7 years have adults enjoyed.

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Now that my kids are grown, I have missed many animated movies released in the past few years. As they were growing up there were many animated movies that I enjoyed watching (Toy Story, Up, Wall-e) as an adult.

&#x200B;

What movies do you recommend I should catch up on.

&#x200B;

And I am looking forward to Toy Store 5 even if I don't have young kids.

Update: Thanks everyone for the wonderful suggestions. I enjoyed watching Wild Robot tonight!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Wild Robot 💯
2. Nimona (2023)

Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse (2018)

Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse (2023)

Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles: Mutant Mayhem (2023)
3. K pop demon hunters 
4. Hoppers
5. The Mitchells vs. the Machines (2021)

Ron's Gone Wrong (2021)
6. Isle of Dogs - stop motion Wes Anderson

Zootopia
7. Marcel the Shell with Shoes On (2021)

Flow (2024)
  - Came here to suggest Flow. Loved it.
8. Wallace and Gromit Vengeance Most Fowl 
9. I really liked turning red

## 3. Swedish actor Stellan Skarsgård turns 75 today. Happy birthday to him!

**Score:** 921

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. And yeah, it's all his children

https://preview.redd.it/xw0jaoei027h1.jpeg?width=630&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=ec7504af13eddba751e89bcce6b4ea675352a451
  - He bred well ..    seriously though Alexander is awesome . I don’t think he ever takes any photo op seriously . Good for him

2. His role Chernobyl is chef kiss. The growth between his character and Harris in that series is something that’s not discussed enough. 
  - Yeah, *Chernobyl* is one of the best series put to film, imo. Imminently rewatchable, too. The supporting actor who played the head miner is one of those guys I look for in a movie cast. Speaks to quality of the production staff. 🚀 EDIT: Alex Ferns. 🛸

4. He was perfect in Andor. He delivered the most moving performance, though he'd just had a stroke and couldn't read a script, so his lines had to be read to him via earphone. Happy Birthday, Stellan!
5. Great versatile actor, can do everything from singing in Mamma Mia! To Dune. Always happy to watch him in anything he is in! 
6. A national treasure. Glad födelsedag!
7. He is a terric actor. Many happy returns.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Rewatched Black Narcissus (1947) again and it really reads almost as a Science Fiction Noir

**Score:** 51
**Body:** The 4K was beautiful, up close to the screen. Closeups impeccably shot, luminous light everywhere. Extraordinary matte painting use (that felt Blade Runner-esque) to create vistas and otherworldly Himalayan precipices. A lot of this is colonialist exoticism, and doesn't read great today, but if you widen the view, the premise is that this place is so high, so wind-blown, so close to the "bare goddess" that it disturbs the mind. The bulwark of nun Catholicism, English education and modern medicine doesn't stand a chance against the pervading, overly sensuous, disorienting "divine". Every time I watch it though it just feels like a Science Fiction film, with the Himalayas presented as another planet (maybe something from C.S. Lewis's Space Trilogy themes?), so alien that the handful of Earthlings that have landed just are way over their heads. It becomes a psychological and moral study, exploring the human relationship to sensuality and moral grounding, and possibly to Being itself. While partaking in a colonialist framework, it also poses a critique of the notion of "civilizing" itself. The use of the feminine double to show a split in the psyche (the good girl / bad girl a common trope in Film Noir) truly intensifies in the final act of the film in spectacular fashion, with some of the more memorable frames in cinema . It's not a Noir proper (probably?), but I'd say a Noir-ish treatment of a color-bleeding psychological thriller. I would consider it a companion film to the color Noir Leave Her To Heave (1945), to which it has some structural parallel and color intent.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Maliciously characterizing Powell's anti-realist Expressionism as colonialist orientalism and comparing its aesthetic to the genre tropes of sci-fi really misses the mark. Is the Red Shoes a work of insensitive ballerina exploitation too? Almost all of his films very intentionally use unrealistic sets, color palettes, and lighting because they are works of highly abstracted art exploring profound psychological themes instead of an attempt to portray the mundane world. Your harping on colonialism sounds like you're trying to make some kind of virtue of being offended on behalf of others (who probably aren't even offended). Moralizing about artists of the past not living up to today's neo-puritanical pieties is silly and hopefully this fad will die soon--it has no place in serious art criticism. The message of the film, like its source book, is anti-colonialist through and through. The nuns look like fools trying to save people that are much saner and more grounded than themselves. The femme fatale aspects and the shooting techniques of the violent climax do indeed seem to borrow from the noir aesthetic. Although there are parallels with Leave Her to Heaven (which everyone should watch), John Huston's Night of the Iguana is overall a much closer film in themes--also Deborah Kerr is great in both.

## 2. Just watched Hitchcock’s Vertigo (1958) for the first time. As a lonely guy about to turn 30, it felt like a mirror to my own life and obsessions.

**Score:** 22
**Body:** This movie has a really depressing vibe in general and has completely unraveled me. Its been a long time since a film has struck such a deep emotional chord. 

Though I have reservations about that far-fetched twist in the plot, believing the film would have been more powerful had it remained in the realm of a surreal dream and ended as a pure psychological mindfuck, as a man in his late twenties wrestling with loneliness, I saw myself in the protagonist.

This movie is a mirror to my life. I consider myself as a generally charming, emotional, and attractive, yet I find no luck in love. I often attract women who are below my standards, while those rare, idealized beauties consume me with obsession. That very obsession always drives them away, leaving me in a state of depression and longing. I went through a painful rejection with a girl who was the epitome of an angel to me, afterward, I dreamed of her every single night for two and a half months. Much like the restaurant scene in the film, I kept seeing her face in strangers. I am also aware that I am held back by so called Madonna-whore complex and all kinds of Freudian elements. 

As a massive fan of David Lynch, Vertigo spellbound me with its dreamlike, mysterious atmosphere. Living with this intensity of feeling can be isolating though. While most people around me function in a dry, sterile realism, I remain trapped in my own world of dreams and shadows.

There is much more to this movie than it meets the eye, thats why It's still so highly regarded. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Are you having some counselling, brother? Sounds like you have some issues to work through. Fortunately you are already good at articulating them, which is a great place to start from.
2. >While most people around me function in a dry, sterile realism

Please don't assume that about people. Other people are not NPC's, they are three dimensional human beings with their own wants, desires, dreams, and shadows. You just don't see them and assume you're special. You're not. You're just a dude in his 20's with big feelings and no tools to express them.

I agree with you that the movie seems like a mirror into your life, in that both you and Scottie suffer from the same problem - you fall in love with the fictional idea of a woman rather than the actual woman herself. You have "standards" that women must meet for you, and if they don't they're not worth your scrutiny. And when you meet a woman who meets your stupid arbitrary standards, instead of relating to her as a fellow human being you put her on a pedestal and obsess about her as some kind of trophy or art object. Other women are human beings! Just like you! Just like everyone else!

I encourage you to read Roger Ebert's article on Vertigo: [https://www.rogerebert.com/reviews/great-movie-vertigo-1958](https://www.rogerebert.com/reviews/great-movie-vertigo-1958) \- he makes an important point, which is that Scottie doesn't fall in love with Madeleine, he falls in love with a fictional creation, and what hurts the most is that the fictional creation isn't even his own, it is the creation of another man. And when he has the opportunity to actually meet the real woman behind the illusion, he refuses the real woman and instead attempts to transform her into the illusion. And by preferring the illusion to the real thing, he has lost both.

In your case, your "standards" are also not your own. Like Scottie's obsession was first created by Gavin, your standards also have been shaped for you by other men, by the culture of masculinity and toxicity that men have built, to tell women that they are only valuable if men find them attractive, and to tell you that women exist for you to worship and obsess over as objects of beauty and pefection, not as three dimensional human beings. Toxic masculinity hurts men just as much as it hurts women.

So you gotta ask yourself - Where did you hear these messages that have led to your Madonna/whore complex and your other shadows? From family members? Friends? Movies? Podcasts? Reddit? 4chan? Pickup artists trying to sell you something? You were misled, friend, by someone who wanted something from you. Movies are art, but they are also commerce, and the studios who make them want you to feel a certain way. Those corporations, those websites and podcasts, even those family and friends, those people and places want you lonely, they want you tired and upset and in the shadows, because then you might buy the thing they're selling you, or re-engage on the website, or listen to the next episode, or reinforce the family dogma, or respect them as a top dog in the friend group. Who misled you? What did they want from you? Did they have your best interests in mind, or were they trying to manipulate you to make money or build their own status?

I strongly, STRONGLY encourage you to interrogate your own feelings about women and other people.  No one is an NPC living an unexamined life. We ALL have our demons and struggles and dimensionality, and women do too. They're not here to meet or break your standards, they're not here for you to admire or obsess over. They are human beings who want to connect with other human beings and create some sort of happiness with them. When you decide to relate to other people as peers and equals as opposed to fictional idealized playthings, then you will find the connection and happiness you long for.

Also consider that Vertigo is Hitchcock confessing about his own treatment of women (which was famously problematic) - it's him saying "hi, I'm the asshole here" - he's not hoping you identify with Scottie , he's hoping you see how delusional he is and by extension how delusional Hitch is, and judge him accordingly. Vertigo is a tragedy, because you can see Scottie's downfall coming a mile away, and all you can do is witness it in horror.

As long as you continue to think of women in the way you do you will never be happy. Instead you will descend into more loneliness, resentment, and incel-like behavior. There's enough of that in this world, and certainly enough of that in cinema. Real connection is possible, but it requires you to change your mindset, get your mind right, and open your heart to the deep reality of other people being just like you. Choose differently, please. Good luck.
  - Very good post, thanks.
    - Thank you - Vertigo is one of my all-time favorite movies, I've seen it maybe once a year for 3 decades. I confess it rang VERY differently to me in my 20's than it does now that I'm 45. There is a romanticism about the film, the dream logic and surrealism and phenomenal score that haunts and lingers in your brain. The way the movie is so passionate and explosive, it's easy to get swept up in the poetry of it. It's only after you've grown a bit that you realize what it's really trying to tell you.

I hope OP is able to work through their worldview a bit. Not only will he be happier and more connected to the world, he will also appreciate this amazing movie even more. That toxic worldview is so limiting, you miss so much of what Hitchcock was trying to do. It would be a shame if OP only ever got to experience a surface-level understanding of the film.
  - I enjoyed your impassioned post. You're spot on about warning to build fantasy castles in our minds in how we relate to the world. Like filters, they inhibit the perception of what is in front of you. Thinking of yourself as special is just another filter that alienates you from people. Life is special. And other people can be special to you. To think of yourself as special is a very dangerous place to be in.

Idealisim is fine and dandy, but it needs to be checked by reality. In saying this, I think it's okay to have standards, expectations in a partner. No one should just take whatever comes along their way. But you also need to ask yourself: Am I expecting too much? And what do I have to offer to enrich the other person's life?

And, in terms of how standards get formed and passed around: Yeah, movies are a great propaganda machine to sow dissatisfaction and keep men and women delusional about relationships. Porn is even worse. Turns sex into sport, where only hot people with stellar physique participate. For the rest, unattainable, so the messaging.

There are two things for men to do that I see:

1. Work on your personality, do therapy if necessary. Some hard facts: You don't need love to be happy. Aaron Beck was the first to say that love is not a human need. It runs very contrary to the mainstream messaging. But it's very liberating once you realize you can be alone and perfectly happy and joyous without anyone. This is powerful because you stop being so needy.
2. Become socially skillful. Go out, meet strangers, talk to them. Talk to women. Learn how you can be a good sport around to be. Learn how to have fun. This is particularly important for the "deep" man, as OP identifies with. Deep relationships are only formed when you start out superficially first. That's why small talk is an important skill. But men want to be deep right away with the woman, and push her away by doing this. [Lighten up, Francis!](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iN-aXzpQUdw)
  - Just want to recognize this as an extremely high-quality post
3. Just stop being weird bro, women can smell that from a mile away regardless of how attractive and awesome you think you are. And lower your expectations. There are plenty of amazing girls out there you might be compatible with that you’re disregarding because of these dumb fantasies 
  - ahhh but remember you’re just advising from your sterile realism while OP lives in a realm of dreams and shadows far beyond your pedestrian observations. /s
4. Assuming you also highly associate with Dostoyevsky’s Notes from Underground,  White Nights or Osamu Dazai’s No Longer Human? Please don’t. They are warnings against this type of thought. They are not supposed to be relatable characters and if they are, it’s a sign to change. 
5. I don’t subscribe to the idea that  what you describe about yourself is the case of Scottie, as well.   There are two lungs to the “problem with Scottie,” and it’s arguably only one of them that fits this reading of “look what men do to women in our society.”

The empathic reading of Scottie is a non-gendered one: limerence and romantic love, fed by a sense of mystery and sudden loss.  Ghosting can be haunting because it is unresolved.  So, obviously, can any sudden loss (the breakup one didn’t see coming; the sudden death of the other).    

In fact, if you haven’t seen “Hiroshima, Mon Amour,” I would recommend it, as it likewise explores limerence and grief, and a sort of transference (Hiroshima = lost lover) but through the eyes of a female protagonist.  

The sympathetic reading of Scottie is a non-gendered one:   mystery as feeding desire (for many women, for example, it’s the attractive guy who says little and is, indeed, “mysterious”); it could even be wandering eyes in a subway car.  There’s also the hook of wanting to save someone who seems endangered— and this, again, can be experienced by either gender, such as the case of a woman in love with a man who has “problems” (drug addiction, anger management, depression), or a woman in love with a woman, or man in love with a woman or another man (trans or cis, or non-binary, etc.).  

Then there’s the factor of *loss* and the fact that Scottie feels guilty about not having been able to save her.   For this, “Ordinary People” would be a relevant companion film to watch.    Empathy is *not* dangerous, here, given what Scottie, as a protagonist, experienced.   It’s this aspect of his “predicament” that’s likewise mirrored by “Hiroshima, Mon Amour”— sudden, unresolved loss with no answers.  Arguably, in the case of Scottie,  survivor’s guilt.

Now, in the *second* half of Vertigo, I do think we have Hitchcock confronting himself and the way in which he dominated his starlets and, it’s fair to say, remade them in the image of his own ideal.   That’s a valid lens through which to look at Scottie, but *his* predicament is not necessarily that of the average man who behaves thus.

For one, there’s the cognitive dissonance over the fact that Judy *is* Madeleine.  The film always works as an emblem of being haunted, or hung up, and gravitating towards those who remind us of the one we lost.  That part, as well, is non-gendered.   It’s *not* unusual for to hear something like “Betty never got over this guy and it’s awkward because this 
new guy looks strikingly similar to her ex.”   In Scottie’s case, it’s even more of a mind-fuck:  this *is* a doppelganger of Madeleine, who has, in fact, identical DNA.   I don’t take plot as the final word on a film’s resonances, but I don’t find that this detail is something to be hand-waved away.  

Now, the way in which Scottie pushes her: the hair like this; the dress just so; the shoes. For someone like Hitchcock, or like many of us (modern-day heterosexual men),  that’s more than too close for comfort.  Scottie, within the plot of the film, has external circumstances — including grief over the death of a woman that he had reason to believe *was* the real Madeleine—  that condition him to yet to recapture an actual dead love.   Being extremely hard on Scottie, given the plot, seemed misplaced and lacking in empathy.  But I do believe that Hitchcock was being hard on *himself*; and it does hold a mirror to viewers, I think.   

Again, though, I think “Hiroshima, Mon Amour” is a “perfect” companion piece to this film.   Sudden loss and unresolved grief; not having “closure” or emotionally satisfying answers; and, even, the sort of “emotional transference” where one person acts as a stand-in for another.  We see this in life:  “you remind me of myself at your age”; “or of the son I never had”; “or of the son I lost; you remind me of him.”    The sexist and domineering angle of “Vertigo” is real but doesn’t exhaust the character of Scottie, for me, nor the film, itself.  It also has lessons that preclude easy moralizing:  a Marilyn Monroe that doesn’t feel valued as herself, but also as her persona (yet who leans into that persona).  Even late comedian Chris Farley, frankly, who — in a non-romantic context— was afraid that, if he lost weight, audiences would no longer find him funny.  There’s a lot.

## 3. Question about ending of Brutalist (spoilers)

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I thought this seemed strongly indicated by the film, but I have not read about it anywhere else.

Zsofia’s adult daughter at the end is Harry’s kid. 

She is played by young Zsofia (Raffey Cassidy) but slightly made up - they added the distinctive eyebrows of Harry (Joe Alwyn).

This also explains why Zsofia rushed to get married right after her encounter with Harry when she previously gave no sign of interest in starting a family, so the husband would believe he got her pregnant.

Does this make sense?  


  
P eep

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Interesting. I hadn't thought about it. It does fit with how sudden the plot turn is, though I read that scene as much more about the rise of Israel in the story, as a location of hope after the betrayals of the American Dream. I can't remember if there was time that passed between those events though. Were they very close in time?
  - Just checked and the encounter scene ends around 2:10. The first scene with her husband is at 2:26. It’s Zsofia’s next significant scene after the encounter. Chronologically in the film, it’s not entirely clear but could be a few months later as not a lot of progress on the building is shown during that time.
    - Yeah, I felt a lot of (chronological) time had passed, tbh. But, I also wasn't paying that much attention to it either. You do bring up an interesting interpretation. 

## 4. Thoughts on William Lustig?

**Score:** 5
**Body:** I recently watched Maniac Cop and Vigilante, and I found myself pleasantly surprised, particularly with Maniac Cop, because as a kid I always just thought it was some terrible direct to video horror movie and so never checked it out. 

&#x200B;

But with both films I was surprised at the skill of his filmmaking. In particular, I love the way he frames shots and has such simple confidence behind the camera in his tracking shots. 

&#x200B;

I was actually watching Vigilante last night and I've kind of come to the position that he is sort of a grindhouse Martin Scorsese if I can be allowed to make such a bold statement. 

&#x200B;

Would love to know your thoughts on Lustig too. 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I went to a Maniac Q&A with him a few months ago at the IFC in Manhattan. Lustig in person is an absolute charmer, clearly delighted to be among fans. 

He wanted to keep the Q&A going. He talked about stealing shots in the Columbus Circle station, ripping off Repulsion, asking Roger Ebert to sign his poster after he trashed the movie, how the movie almost starred Daria Nicolodi, and how lame modern serial killers are compared to their 70s golden era. Son of Sam and John Wayne Gacy and others who were models for this character all had mommy issues, he said. "A lot of bad mommies out there." One of the most entertaining director Q&As I've seen.

His Blue Underground label has put out some nice editions of grindhouse movies, so that's another reason to like him.
